chapters.title: Breathe to live well and live to breathe well
chapters.text:
James Nestor's life was falling apart. He was dealing with heavy stress and the after effects of a bad case of pneumonia. His doctor suggested he try a breathing class. That is how his journey started.

The teachers at the class led their students through a method called Sudarshan Kriya. The students were asked to close their eyes, breathe in slowly through the nose, and breathe out slowly, all while keeping their focus on the breath. The teacher explained that each breath in brings fresh energy, and each breath out clears out the old, stale energy. At first, Nestor did not notice any change. By the end of the session, though, he felt very hot, as if he had just run a long race. The next day, he felt calm and relaxed.

It turns out that the art of breathing can do remarkable things with the body and with human ability. Nestor met free divers who could plunge to depths of thirty to ninety meters on a single breath. Breathing was their medicine and a way to reach new limits. He also found breathing techniques in ancient Chinese texts dating back to 400 BC. They said breath could destroy or heal. Many doctors and scientists still believe that air just keeps us alive and offers nothing special.

The best way to prevent many chronic problems, lift athletic performance, and add years to life is to focus on how we breathe. That means learning to balance oxygen and carbon dioxide in the body by inhaling and exhaling slowly. As James Nestor has noted, the right kind of breathing pays off in many ways.

Even so, the way most of us breathe is getting worse every year. Poor breathing can lead to many health issues, both physical and mental. The good news is that a few simple changes can help.

In modern society, about 90 percent of people do not breathe the way the body was designed to.

chapters.title: Why humans ended up with the worst airways
chapters.text:
Nestor had breathing trouble for as long as he could remember. His airways were a mess, the bones of his skull, mouth, and throat had all shrunk and shifted. That history led him to Dr. Jayakar Nayak and an unusual experiment. In the first part of the trial, he would wear silicone plugs in his nose, forcing him to breathe only through his mouth. In the second part, he would breathe through his nose, do breathing exercises, and let the doctors record how his body changed.

The urge to breathe through the mouth kicks in when the nose is not working well. The nose cleans the air, removing dust and germs, and warms and moistens it before it reaches the lungs. When breathing goes wrong, the inside of the nose shrinks, which makes the airway even tighter. That tighter space lets bacteria grow, which leads to more infections and more colds.

Of all the animals on the planet, humans deal with the most breathing trouble.

Oxygen is the fuel of life. But our airways have been on a long slide. About one and a half million years ago, the structure of the human face began to shift. Evolution gave us a nose to heat and clean the air. Compared to us, our ancestors did not snore or stop breathing in their sleep. Their airways were wide, their jaws strong, and their teeth lined up neatly, even without a toothbrush in sight.

Over time, the way we prepare food changed. Cooking and processing softened what we ate, and the muscles of the face grew weaker. The brain grew larger, taking up more space in the skull. The nose we were left with was less able to filter the air. The body became more prone to illness. Misaligned teeth became the norm rather than the exception. Those changes may have helped us rise to the top of the food chain, but they also made us more fragile.

Just before the experiment started, Nestor met a fellow volunteer, Anders Olsson. They moved into the same neighborhood, kept track of their health numbers, and did workouts and breath drills together. Olsson pushed for bike rides in both parts of the trial to test stamina. But even a quiet ride felt rough for Nestor while he was breathing through his mouth. His energy bottomed out fast.

chapters.title: The hidden cost of mouth breathing
chapters.text:
Athletes tend to know a lot about breathing, because it sits at the heart of any workout. The body pulls energy from air and food in two main ways, aerobic, with oxygen, and anaerobic, without air. Anaerobic power, which comes from breaking down glucose, is less efficient than aerobic and stresses the body, leaving you weak and dizzy. You have probably felt that after a hard session. As the body warms up, it shifts into aerobic mode and handles stress more easily.

Coach Phil Maffetone, who worked with top athletes in the 1970s, designed a personal training plan built around heart rate. With his plan, athletes stayed in the aerobic zone, trained smarter, and bounced back faster. A simple way to find your target heart rate is to subtract your age from 180. Stay near that number through your workouts, and you stay in the aerobic zone.

Meanwhile, Nestor's mouth breathing experiment rolled on. He was always tired. His sleep, snoring, and apnea markers got worse. He also noticed his thinking had dulled. A study in Japan on rats backs this up. When the rats were forced to breathe through their mouths, they had a much harder time getting through a maze. The same goes for humans. Many people try to mouth breathe during exercise, which makes cardio harder and more draining.

Nestor also recalls Egil P. Harvold's experiment with monkeys. He placed silicone plugs in the noses of wild primates. Over two years, their muzzles became deformed. When Harvold removed the plugs, the animals relearned to breathe through their noses, and their jaws returned to their old shape. Normal nasal breathing brought them back to a happy life.

Eventually, James Nestor pulled the plugs out. At home, he rinsed his sinuses and breathed in deeply through his nose, taking in every smell. In that moment, he saw how much proper, full breathing matters.

Breathing is more than air moving in and out. It is a way of connecting with the world around you.

chapters.title: Why breathing matters in sport and life
chapters.text:
Athletes tend to know more about breath than the rest of us, because oxygen and effort are so tightly linked. There are two ways the body pulls energy from food and air. The aerobic path uses oxygen. The anaerobic path does not. The second path is a side effect of burning sugar without air. It is less efficient and leaves the body drained, light headed, and slow to recover. You have probably felt it after a hard burst of effort. As the body warms up, it shifts into the aerobic mode and stress feels easier to take.

Coach Phil Maffetone, who trained top athletes in the 1970s, built a personal program around heart rate. Athletes who trained in the aerobic zone could go longer, recover faster, and avoid the wall of fatigue. His shortcut for finding your training zone is simple. Take 180 and subtract your age. Stay under that number during exercise, and you will stay in the aerobic band.

While Nestor was running his own mouth breathing experiment, the cost was clear. He felt tired all the time. His sleep got worse. His snoring grew louder. He could think less clearly. A study in Japan found the same in rats. When rats were forced to breathe through their mouths, they ran mazes more slowly. The lesson holds for humans. Many gym goers and weekend warriors breathe through the mouth when they push themselves. Cardio becomes harder than it needs to be.

Nestor also pointed to an older study by Egil P. Harvold with young monkeys. Plugs were placed in their noses. Over two years, the bones of the face shifted. When the plugs came out, the monkeys learned to breathe through the nose again and the faces snapped back. Open nasal breathing brought them back to a calm, healthy life.

In the end, Nestor pulled his own plugs out. At home, he rinsed his sinuses and breathed in deeply through the nose, taking in the smell of the room. In that moment, he understood how much depends on how we breathe.

Breath is not just air in and out. It is a link between the body and the world around it.

chapters.title: Carbon dioxide is more useful than most people think
chapters.text:
The more efficient the lungs, the longer people live. Lung capacity grows with physical activity. Free divers, for example, can expand their lung capacity by thirty to forty percent, and regular cycling can lift it by about fifteen percent.

Lung capacity is a strong signal of how long a person will live.

In the 1940s, conductor Carl Stough taught his choir to exhale better by training the breathing muscles. After a few sessions, the singers' voices grew stronger and clearer. One day, he got a call from the tuberculosis ward at a veterans' hospital. The patients had emphysema, a breakdown of lung tissue. Stough realized the problem was that they could not exhale the stale air. He also saw that their diaphragms were very weak, which slowed and weakened the airflow.

Stough worked with the patients by massaging their necks and chests during slow exhales, trying to wake up the diaphragm. He could not cure emphysema, because it is irreversible. But as lung capacity grew, the patients found it easier to breathe and move. One 55 year old man was well enough to leave the hospital and sail off on a yacht. Stough showed that a better exhale could change a patient's life.

Carbon dioxide is another quiet player in the body's health. Anders Olsson argued that raising its level in the body can help with weight loss and may lower the risk of disease. Carbon dioxide helps the body get more oxygen by opening blood vessels, so more oxygen reaches the cells. Fast breathing pushes carbon dioxide out of the tissues, cutting blood flow to the muscles and organs and causing weakness and dizziness. Slow breathing keeps oxygen and carbon dioxide in a healthy balance.

Olsson showed the trade off in action. He slowed his breathing to six breaths a minute, breathing in through the nose and out through the mouth. His carbon dioxide level rose to a healthy number, and his oxygen level stayed high. James Nestor tried the same technique and found that slow breathing lifted his endurance, which made his workouts more effective.

The lungs lose about twelve percent of their volume between the ages of thirty and fifty.

chapters.title: How modern habits hurt the way we breathe
chapters.text:
In recent decades, two trends have taken hold. People eat more, and they breathe more often. Around a quarter of the population now lives with chronic issues linked to over breathing. The good news is that exercises can train the body to breathe less, which is different from breathing slower. By getting the body used to a smaller breath, you raise the ceiling for oxygen use, and that builds both endurance and a longer life.

The point of the drill is to feel good while the level of carbon dioxide in the blood rises. Over time, that lets the body absorb oxygen more efficiently when it really needs it.

In the 1950s, a Ukrainian lung doctor named Konstantin Buteyko built a method around the conscious refusal to over breathe. He ran a large trial and taught patients to limit their breath. Both healthy and sick volunteers felt better. Their blood pressure dropped. Their migraines faded. Try a basic Buteyko drill, the Control Pause.

Sit up tall with a straight back. Pinch both nostrils shut with your fingers. Exhale softly out of the mouth, all the way. Start a timer and hold the breath. When the body asks for air, stop the timer and breathe in slowly through the nose. Rest, then repeat.

Asthma is another modern plague. The airways tighten and spasm when irritants like dust or cold air come in. Too much breathing can also trigger an attack. Nestor met David Wiebe, a 58 year old maker of cellos and violins, whose asthma was severe. Three months of breath drills let him cut his inhaler use to once a day, and his symptoms nearly vanished.

Misaligned jaws are one more common problem. As humans learned to grow food, the shape of the mouth began to change, and the airways narrowed. Over time, the issue has become widespread. Processed food stripped of minerals and vitamins plays a role. Early in the 20th century, dentist Weston Price focused on curing the modern mouth. He found that a lack of vitamins and minerals drove much of the damage. But he missed a key piece. Chewing. As food grew softer, the jaws stopped working hard, and the face changed faster. James Nestor's tip is simple. Chew gum to give the jaw a workout.

Hard chewing wakes up the stem cells, which can help the face look younger and the airways open wider.

chapters.title: Widen the airways and find Breathing Plus
chapters.text:
Many modern people have a deviated septum or other nasal issues. In some cases, they turn to surgery, but surgeons can sometimes remove too much tissue. The nose loses its ability to filter and moisten the air, leading to empty nose syndrome. Oddly, too much air can leave people feeling like they cannot breathe. So, Nestor decided not to fix his nose with surgery, and instead turned to the mouth.

The blockage of the airways is closely tied to the size of the mouth. The smaller the mouth, the more the tongue, uvula, and other tissues crowd the airway. In Nestor's view, modern orthodontics often makes the problem worse by shrinking the oral cavity even more. Many doctors disagree, but dentist Mike Mew believes the mouth can be reshaped through breathing exercises. His approach is called mewing, and videos on YouTube walk through the steps. In short, close your mouth so your teeth barely touch. Press your tongue against the roof of the mouth, and keep your head straight. When you sit, your spine should look a little like the letter J. Use your abdominal muscles to breathe.

James Nestor also tried an expander designed by Dr. Theodore Belfor. It does not straighten teeth, but it encourages the mouth to widen and the jaw to work harder. After using it, Nestor's lower jaw sat in a healthier position, and the infection in his maxillary sinuses cleared up.

Nestor also lays out the practical exercises he calls Breathing Plus. They are about controlling the breath and consciously putting the body in a state of stress or relaxation, depending on what you need. Breathing can serve both poles. During the civil war, for example, soldiers in large numbers suffered from diarrhea and a racing heart. Their nervous systems were stuck in overdrive. The body's emergency system, the sympathetic side, was running hot, while the relaxation side, the parasympathetic, was not pulling its weight. Learning to control your breathing gives you a handle on even the most intense stress.

You can take charge of your breathing and decide when the body needs stress and when it needs rest.

chapters.title: Three breath methods you can try at home
chapters.text:
James Nestor wanted to test the stressful breath method on himself. The method, Tummo, trains the body to bend and flex in the face of stress. A simple Tummo routine looks like this.

Grab a pillow and rest your head so the body can let go. Take 30 quick, deep breaths through the nose, filling the belly first. As you exhale, empty the belly, then the chest. After the 30th breath, exhale naturally, leaving a little air in the lungs, and hold the breath for as long as you can. Breathe in and hold for 15 seconds. Exhale gently. Repeat the cycle at least twice.

Pair the breath with cold. A few cold exposures a week can sharpen the effect. Wim Hof, who made Tummo famous in the West, could sit in an ice bath for close to two hours and run a marathon in 40 degree heat without a sip of water. He used breath to reset the body's energy.

Breath builds trust between mind and body.

A more intense version of Tummo was developed by Czech psychiatrist Stanislav Grof. It uses fast, deep breathing paired with loud, psychedelic music. The method has produced mystical visions in patients, and some have even reported feeling the soul leave the body.

Ancient Chinese writers advised holding the breath to chase away fear. They saw it as a way to quiet the mind and ease worry.

The Master of Great Nothing, of Sung Shan, wrote that the strongest signal of how long a person would live was not genes, diet, or even daily exercise. It was lung capacity.

The third Breath Plus method is Sudarshan Kriya. It uses breath to guide the life force, called prana, through the body. The practice takes real commitment and runs in four phases.

Mantra. Limited breath, short inhale, long exhale. Controlled breath, inhale and hold for four seconds, exhale for six, hold for two. A final phase of intense breath for 40 seconds.

There are many breath methods that can lift how you feel. Pick one that calls to you and track what it does. Or try a few and stick with the one that gives you the clearest lift.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
The possibilities that breath opens are wide and striking. Still, James Nestor points out that breathing is not a cure for everything. It will not replace antibiotics when a doctor prescribes them. It does, however, help with asthma, stress, autoimmune conditions, and headaches. Breathing also helps keep the body in balance and can stop small issues from turning into bigger ones. The practice works best when paired with a healthy diet, regular movement, and good stress management.

Make the following rules a part of your daily routine and feel a real shift in your life.

Try to breathe through your mouth as little as possible. Air should enter through the nose, where it gets filtered, humidified, and warmed.
Breathe through the nose, and savor each smell. Air that has traveled through the nose is better for you.
Make every exhale count. Push as much air out of the body as you can, slowly and fully.
Do not forget the mouth. Strong jaws help the airways. Chew a lot, and do not be afraid to add tough, raw foods to your meals.
Try to master Tummo, the intense breathing method. Make friends with your breath and the air around you.
Hold your breath to ease anxiety.
Take care of yourself. Make breathing practice a daily habit. In the early days, do not push long breath holds. If you feel lightheaded while breathing in and out slowly, stop and rest.

A small step to try.

If you struggle with nasal stuffiness, try the following method.

Sit up straight, exhale freely, and pinch both nostrils.
When you feel the urge to breathe, inhale slowly through the nose.
Continue the controlled breathing for up to a minute.
Repeat the exercise six times.

If you have read this far, the work has already started.
